---
title: "Applied Bayesian Methods"
subtitle: "***DAY 1:** Theoretical introduction and multi-level models*"
author: "Alexander Massey"
date: today
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
# html-math-method: katex
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    theme: white
    css: assets/custom.scss
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: false
    incremental: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    fontsize: 2em
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-image: ""
    katex: TRUE
include-after-body: assets/logo-inject.html
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# What is Bayesian Inference? {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## The core idea

> "The approach is based upon the idea that the experimenter begins with some prior beliefs about the system, and then updates these beliefs on the basis of observed data."
> — Gimenez (2025)

. . .

We want to answer: *how plausible is some hypothesis given the data?*

$$\Pr(\text{hypothesis} \mid \text{data}) = \;?$$

. . .

The answer is **Bayes' theorem** — and it falls directly out of the definition of conditional probability

---

## Conditional Probability — A Reminder

$\Pr(A \mid B)$ is the probability of $A$ **given that** $B$ has occurred

. . .

By definition:

$$\Pr(A \mid B) = \frac{\Pr(A \cap B)}{\Pr(B)}$$

. . .

The same joint probability $\Pr(A \cap B)$ can be factored two ways:

$$\Pr(A \cap B) = \Pr(B \mid A)\,\Pr(A) = \Pr(A \mid B)\,\Pr(B)$$

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
This symmetry is all we need — **Bayes' theorem is a direct consequence of these two factorisations being equal**
:::

---

## Bayes' Theorem — Derived from First Principles

Starting from the two factorisations of the joint probability:

$$\Pr(B \mid A)\,\Pr(A) = \Pr(A \mid B)\,\Pr(B)$$

. . .

Divide both sides by $\Pr(B)$:

$$\boxed{\Pr(A \mid B) = \frac{\Pr(B \mid A)\,\Pr(A)}{\Pr(B)}}$$

. . .

That is all it is. No new axioms — just rearranging the definition of conditional probability.

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
The ordering matters: $\Pr(A \mid B) \neq \Pr(B \mid A)$ in general — this asymmetry is exactly what makes the theorem useful
:::


# The Classic Example: Diagnostic Testing {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## Setup

*(Adapted from Held, Likelihood and Bayesian Inference)*

A diagnostic test has **90% sensitivity** and **90% specificity**:

$$\Pr(T^+ \mid D^+) = 0.9 \qquad \Pr(T^- \mid D^-) = 0.9$$

- $T^+$: positive test result; $\quad D^+$: person has the disease
- **Sensitivity**: the test correctly detects disease 90% of the time
- **Specificity**: the test correctly rules out disease 90% of the time

. . .

Suppose disease **prevalence** is $\Pr(D^+) = 0.01$

. . .

**Question:** given a positive test, what is the probability of actually having the disease?

$$\Pr(D^+ \mid T^+) = \;?$$

---

## Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1.** Compute $\Pr(T^+)$ via the **law of total probability**:

$$
\Pr(T^+) =
{\color{blue}{\Pr(T^+ \mid D^+)}}\,{\color{blue}{\Pr(D^+)}}
+
{\color{red}{\Pr(T^+ \mid D^-)}}\,{\color{red}{\Pr(D^-)}}
$$

. . .

$$
= {\color{blue}{0.9 \times 0.01}}
+
{\color{red}{(1 - 0.9) \times 0.99}}
= 0.009 + 0.099 = 0.108
$$

. . .

**Step 2.** Apply Bayes' theorem:

$$\Pr(D^+ \mid T^+) = \frac{\Pr(T^+ \mid D^+)\,\Pr(D^+)}{\Pr(T^+)} = \frac{0.9 \times 0.01}{0.108} \approx \mathbf{0.083}$$

. . .

::: {.callout-important appearance="minimal"}
A positive test raises disease probability **1.0% → 8.3%** — still low! When disease is rare, false positives from the large healthy population swamp true positives.
:::

---

## Why the Result Feels Surprising

The test has 90% accuracy in both directions. Yet only 1-in-12 positive tests reflects true disease.

. . .

**The numbers:**

| | Has Disease ($D^+$) | No Disease ($D^-$) |
|---|---|---|
| Test positive ($T^+$) | $0.9 \times 0.01 = 0.009$ | $0.1 \times 0.99 = 0.099$ |
| Test negative ($T^-$) | $0.1 \times 0.01 = 0.001$ | $0.9 \times 0.99 = 0.891$ |

. . .

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
**The Bayesian lesson:** the posterior $\Pr(D^+ \mid T^+)$ depends critically on the **prior** $\Pr(D^+)$. Ignore the prior and you get the wrong answer. This is not a statistical trick — it is basic probability.
:::


# From Events to Parameters {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## Extending Bayes' Theorem to Statistical Models

Replace discrete events with a **continuous parameter** $\theta$ and observed **data** $x$:

$$A \to \theta \qquad B \to x$$

. . .

Bayes' theorem becomes:

$$\underbrace{p(\theta \mid x)}_{\text{posterior}} = \frac{\underbrace{p(x \mid \theta)}_{\text{likelihood}} \;\underbrace{p(\theta)}_{\text{prior}}}{\underbrace{p(x)}_{\text{normalising constant}}}$$

. . .

The denominator marginalises over all possible $\theta$:

$$p(x) = \int p(x \mid \theta)\,p(\theta)\,d\theta$$

. . .

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
This integral is the **central computational challenge** — often high-dimensional and analytically intractable. It motivates MCMC.
:::

---

## The Three Components

**Prior** $p(\theta)$

- Encodes beliefs *before* seeing the data
- Can be informative (domain knowledge) or weakly informative
- Source of much philosophical debate — but unavoidable in any inference

. . .

**Likelihood** $p(x \mid \theta)$

- Probability of the observed data as a function of $\theta$
- The same object as in maximum likelihood estimation
- The data enter exclusively through this term

. . .

**Posterior** $p(\theta \mid x)$

- Updated belief *after* seeing the data; a full probability distribution over $\theta$
- The basis for all Bayesian inference and decision-making

---

## The Proportionality Shortcut

Since $p(x)$ does not depend on $\theta$, we often write:

$$p(\theta \mid x) \propto p(x \mid \theta)\; p(\theta)$$

$$\text{Posterior} \propto \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Prior}$$

. . .

> "The density function of the posterior distribution can be obtained through multiplication of the likelihood function and the density function of the prior distribution with subsequent normalisation."
> — Held (2014)

. . .

We work with the un-normalised product and only divide by $p(x)$ at the end — and for **conjugate models** we never need to compute it explicitly at all.


# A Detour: Maximum Likelihood Estimation {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## The Likelihood — A Change of Perspective

The term $p(x \mid \theta)$ appears inside Bayes' theorem. Before using it in a Bayesian analysis, it is worth understanding it on its own.

. . .

Normally we think of $p(x \mid \theta)$ as: *given the parameter, what data should we expect?*

. . .

**Flip the question.** We have observed $x$. Treat data as fixed and ask: *for which $\theta$ were these data most probable?*

$$\mathscr{L}(\theta) = p(x \mid \theta) \qquad \text{(same formula, different interpretation)}$$

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
Same mathematical object — different conceptual role. As a **likelihood**, $\theta$ is the fixed but unknown variable of interest and $x$ is the observed realization of a random process repeated n times.
:::

---

## Worked Example: Binomial Likelihood

**Setup.** A female bird has $k = 3$ successful breeding attempts out of $N = 10$.

Model: $Y \sim \text{Binomial}(N, p)$

. . .

**Likelihood function:**

$$\mathscr{L}(p \mid k, N) = \binom{N}{k} p^k (1-p)^{N-k} = \binom{10}{3} p^3 (1-p)^7$$

. . .

Evaluate at a few candidate values:

| $p$ | $\mathscr{L}(p)$ |
|-----|-----------------|
| 0.10 | 0.057 |
| 0.25 | 0.250 |
| 0.30 | 0.267 |
| 0.90 | 0.000 |

. . .

The data are most compatible with $p$ near 0.3 — but we need calculus to confirm this.

---

## Deriving the MLE analytically

We want $\hat{p} = \arg\max_p \; \mathscr{L}(p)$. Since $\log$ is monotone, maximising $\log \mathscr{L}$ is equivalent.

$$\log \mathscr{L}(p) = \underbrace{\log\binom{N}{k}}_{\text{constant w.r.t. } p} + k\log p + (N-k)\log(1-p)$$

. . .

**Differentiate** with respect to $p$:

$$\frac{d \log \mathscr{L}}{dp} = \frac{k}{p} - \frac{N - k}{1 - p}$$

. . .

**Set equal to zero:**

$$\frac{k}{p} = \frac{N-k}{1-p} \implies k(1-p) = (N-k)\,p \implies k - kp = Np - kp$$

. . .

$$k = Np \implies \boxed{\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{k}{N} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.3}$$

---

## Verify: Second Derivative Confirms a Maximum

$$\frac{d^2 \log \mathscr{L}}{dp^2} = -\frac{k}{p^2} - \frac{N-k}{(1-p)^2}$$

. . .

This is **strictly negative** for all $p \in (0,1)$, $k > 0$, $N > k$ — confirming the critical point is a global maximum.

. . .

The **observed Fisher information** quantifies curvature at the MLE:

$$\mathscr{I}(\hat{p}) = -\frac{d^2 \log \mathscr{L}}{dp^2}\bigg|_{p=\hat{p}} = \frac{N}{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}$$

. . .

The MLE standard error follows as:

$$\text{SE}(\hat{p}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathscr{I}(\hat{p})}} = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{N}}$$

---

## MLE vs. Bayesian: A Comparison

```{=html}
<style>
.compare-table th { background: #2c3e50; color: white; padding: 8px 14px; }
.compare-table td { padding: 7px 14px; vertical-align: top; font-size: 0.85em; }
.compare-table tr:nth-child(even) { background: #f4f4f4; }
.compare-table { border-collapse: collapse; width: 100%; margin-top: 0.5em; }
.tag-bad  { color: #c0392b; font-weight: bold; }
.tag-good { color: #27ae60; font-weight: bold; }
</style>
<table class="compare-table">
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Property</th>
      <th>MLE (Frequentist)</th>
      <th>Bayesian</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Parameter view</strong></td>
      <td>Fixed, unknown constant</td>
      <td>Random variable with a distribution</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Inference output</strong></td>
      <td>Point estimate + SE</td>
      <td>Full posterior distribution</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Uncertainty interval</strong></td>
      <td>Confidence interval (repeated-sampling)</td>
      <td>Credible interval (direct probability)</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Consistency</strong></td>
      <td><span class="tag-bad">Asymptotic only</span></td>
      <td>Valid for any sample size</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Efficiency (Cramér-Rao)</strong></td>
      <td><span class="tag-bad">Asymptotic only</span></td>
      <td>Incorporates prior information</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Normality of estimator</strong></td>
      <td><span class="tag-bad">Asymptotic only</span></td>
      <td>Posterior shape follows the model</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>p-values</strong></td>
      <td><span class="tag-bad">Violates likelihood principle†</span></td>
      <td>Not used; decisions from posterior</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Prior knowledge</strong></td>
      <td>Not explicit</td>
      <td><span class="tag-good">Formally encoded in prior</span></td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td><strong>Main cost</strong></td>
      <td>Optimisation (usually fast)</td>
      <td><span class="tag-bad">Computing normalising constant p(x) is hard!!!</span></td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>
<p style="font-size:0.72em; margin-top:0.6em;">
† p-values condition on H_0 and integrate over <em>unobserved</em> hypothetical datasets,
violating the <strong>likelihood principle</strong>: inference should depend only on the likelihood of what was actually observed.
</p>
```

## Under a uniform prior, the posterior mode equals the MLE.


# Posterior Summaries {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## Summarising the Posterior

Once we have $p(\theta \mid x)$, we extract summaries analogous to descriptive statistics

. . .

**Posterior mean** — minimises squared-error loss:
$$E(\theta \mid x) = \int \theta \, f(\theta \mid x)\, d\theta$$

**Posterior mode (MAP)** — minimises 0–1 loss:
$$\text{Mode}(\theta \mid x) = \arg\max_\theta \; f(\theta \mid x)$$

**Posterior median** — minimises absolute-error loss:
$$\text{Med}(\theta \mid x) : \int_{-\infty}^{a} f(\theta \mid x)\, d\theta = 0.5$$

---

## Bayes Estimators and Loss Functions

A **Bayes estimate** minimises expected posterior loss:

$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_a \; E\bigl[\ell(a, \theta) \mid x\bigr] = \arg\min_a \int \ell(a, \theta)\, f(\theta \mid x)\, d\theta$$

. . .

| Loss function $\ell(a, \theta)$ | Bayes Estimate |
|---|---|
| Quadratic: $(a - \theta)^2$ | Posterior **mean** |
| Absolute: $\lvert a - \theta \rvert$ | Posterior **median** |
| 0–1: $\mathbf{1}(a \neq \theta)$ as $\varepsilon \to 0$ | Posterior **mode** (MAP) |

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
Choice of loss function should reflect the real cost of being wrong in your application — this is a scientific decision, not a statistical default
:::

---

## Credible Intervals

For $\gamma \in (0, 1)$, a $\gamma \times 100\%$ **credible interval** $[t_l, t_u]$ satisfies:

$$\int_{t_l}^{t_u} f(\theta \mid x)\, d\theta = \gamma$$

. . .

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Frequentist 95% CI**

The interval is random — it varies across hypothetical experiments. In the long run, 95% of such intervals contain the true $\theta$. This particular interval either contains it or it doesn't.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Bayesian 95% CrI**

Given the observed data and prior, $\Pr(\theta \in [t_l, t_u] \mid x) = 0.95$. A direct probability statement about where $\theta$ lies.
:::
:::


# Conjugate Priors {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## Why Conjugate Priors?

Recall: $p(x) = \int p(x \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)\, d\theta$ is often intractable.

. . .

**Conjugate priors** are a special case where the integral works out in closed form:

> If the posterior $p(\theta \mid x)$ belongs to the same distributional family as the prior $p(\theta)$, the prior is **conjugate** to the likelihood.

. . .

Benefits:

- We can analytically describe the posterior in closed-form including **update rules**

---

## The Beta-Binomial Model

The canonical conjugate model for a proportion $p \in [0,1]$

**Likelihood:**
$$x \mid p \;\sim\; \text{Binomial}(n,\, p) \qquad \Rightarrow \qquad p(x \mid p) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$$

. . .

**Prior:**
$$p \;\sim\; \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta) \qquad \Rightarrow \qquad p(p) \propto p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1}$$

. . .

**Posterior** — multiply and collect powers of $p$:

$$p(p \mid x) \propto \underbrace{p^x(1-p)^{n-x}}_{\text{likelihood}} \cdot \underbrace{p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1}}_{\text{prior}} = p^{(\alpha+x)-1}(1-p)^{(\beta+n-x)-1}$$

. . .

$$\boxed{p \mid x \;\sim\; \text{Beta}(\alpha + x,\;\; \beta + n - x)}$$

---

## Beta-Binomial: Update Rules and Posterior Mean

The update simply adds observed counts to the prior hyperparameters:

$$\alpha_{\text{post}} = \alpha_{\text{prior}} + \underbrace{x}_{\text{successes}} \qquad \beta_{\text{post}} = \beta_{\text{prior}} + \underbrace{(n - x)}_{\text{failures}}$$

. . .

Think of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ as **pseudo-counts** of prior successes and failures.

. . .

**Posterior mean:**
$$E(p \mid x) = \frac{\alpha + x}{\alpha + \beta + n}$$

. . .

This is a **weighted average** of the prior mean $\dfrac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta}$ and the MLE $\dfrac{x}{n}$ — the prior pulls the estimate toward prior beliefs, especially when $n$ is small.

---

## The Beta Prior — Shape Intuition

$$f(p \mid \alpha, \beta) \propto p^{\alpha - 1}(1 - p)^{\beta - 1}$$

```{r}
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| fig-width: 9
library(ggplot2)
p_seq <- seq(0.001, 0.999, length.out = 500)
params <- list(
  list(a = 1,   b = 1,   lab = "Beta(1, 1) — Uniform (complete ignorance)"),
  list(a = 2,   b = 2,   lab = "Beta(2, 2) — Symmetric, mild"),
  list(a = 5,   b = 2,   lab = "Beta(5, 2) — Skewed toward high p"),
  list(a = 0.5, b = 0.5, lab = "Beta(0.5, 0.5) — U-shaped (bimodal prior)")
)
df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(params, function(pr) {
  data.frame(p = p_seq,
             density = dbeta(p_seq, pr$a, pr$b),
             shape   = pr$lab)
}))
ggplot(df, aes(p, density, colour = shape)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1", name = NULL) +
  labs(x = expression(theta), y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.text = element_text(size = 10))
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
$\alpha = \beta = 1$ is the **uniform prior** — complete ignorance about $p$
:::

---

## Beta-Binomial: Worked Example

**Bird breeding:** $k = 3$ successes out of $N = 10$ attempts. Prior: $\text{Beta}(2, 2)$.

. . .

$$\text{Prior mean} = \frac{2}{2+2} = 0.500 \qquad \text{MLE} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.300$$

. . .

**Posterior:**
$$p \mid x \;\sim\; \text{Beta}(2+3,\; 2+7) = \text{Beta}(5,\; 9)$$

. . .

$$\text{Posterior mean} = \frac{5}{5+9} = \frac{5}{14} \approx 0.357$$

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
The posterior sits **between** the prior mean and the MLE. With only $N = 10$ observations the prior still has influence — equivalent to 4 pseudo-observations of prior data.
:::

---

## Prior Dominance and Data Accumulation

*Watch how the posterior shifts as more coin flips arrive*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Simulate coin flips and watch the posterior update in real time.*

::: ojs-two-col
```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof priorParams = Inputs.form([
  Inputs.range([0.5, 10], {value: 2, step: 0.5, label: "Prior α (Beta)"}),
  Inputs.range([0.5, 10], {value: 2, step: 0.5, label: "Prior β (Beta)"})
])

viewof trueP = Inputs.range([0, 1], {value: 0.5, step: 0.05, label: "True p"})

viewof coinData = {
  let heads = 0
  let total = 0

  const flipBtn = html`<button style="margin:4px 8px 4px 0; padding:6px 14px; font-size:1em;">🪙 Flip Coin</button>`
  const resetBtn = html`<button style="margin:4px 0; padding:6px 14px; font-size:1em;">↺ Reset</button>`
  const status = html`<div style="margin-top:8px; font-size:0.95em;">Flips: 0 | Heads: 0</div>`
  const container = html`<div>${flipBtn}${resetBtn}${status}</div>`

  const dispatch = () => {
    status.textContent = `Flips: ${total} | Heads: ${heads}`
    container.value = { heads, total }
    container.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent("input"))
  }

  flipBtn.onclick = () => {
    const flip = Math.random() < trueP ? 1 : 0
    total += 1
    heads += flip
    dispatch()
  }

  resetBtn.onclick = () => {
    heads = 0
    total = 0
    dispatch()
  }

  container.value = { heads, total }
  return container
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
prior_a = priorParams[0]
prior_b = priorParams[1]
n_obs   = coinData.total
x_obs   = coinData.heads
post_a  = prior_a + x_obs
post_b  = prior_b + (n_obs - x_obs)
theta   = Array.from({length: 300}, (_, i) => i / 299)

function betaPDF(theta, a, b) {
  function lgamma(x) {
    const c = [76.18009172947146,-86.50532032941677,24.01409824083091,
               -1.231739572450155,0.1208650973866179e-2,-0.5395239384953e-5];
    let y = x, tmp = x + 5.5;
    tmp -= (x + 0.5) * Math.log(tmp);
    let ser = 1.000000000190015;
    for (let j = 0; j < 6; j++) ser += c[j] / ++y;
    return -tmp + Math.log(2.5066282746310005 * ser / x);
  }
  const logB = lgamma(a) + lgamma(b) - lgamma(a + b);
  return Math.exp((a - 1) * Math.log(theta) + (b - 1) * Math.log(1 - theta) - logB);
}

priorCurve = theta.slice(1, -1).map(t => ({theta: t, density: betaPDF(t, prior_a, prior_b), curve: "Prior"}))
postCurve  = theta.slice(1, -1).map(t => ({theta: t, density: betaPDF(t, post_a,  post_b),  curve: "Posterior"}))
allCurves  = [...priorCurve, ...postCurve]

Plot.plot({
  width: 600,
  height: 600,
  marginLeft: 50,
  style: { fontSize: "24px" },
  x: {label: "θ", domain: [0, 1], labelOffset: 36},
  y: {label: "Density", nice: true},
  color: {
    legend: true,
    domain: ["Prior", "Posterior"],
    range: ["#7bafd4", "#e07b54"],
    swatchWidth: 30,
    swatchHeight: 18,
    style: {fontSize: "18px"}
  },
  marks: [
    Plot.lineY(allCurves, {x: "theta", y: "density", stroke: "curve", strokeWidth: 6.5}),
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.ruleX([trueP], {stroke: "black", strokeWidth: 3, strokeDasharray: "6,4"}),
    <!-- Plot.ruleX(n_obs > 0 ? [x_obs / n_obs] : [], {stroke: "steelblue", strokeWidth: 3, strokeDasharray: "6,4"}) -->
  ],
  caption: `Flips: ${n_obs}  |  Heads: ${x_obs}  |  Posterior: Beta(${post_a}, ${post_b})  |  Mean = ${(post_a / (post_a + post_b)).toFixed(3)}`
})
```
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Key Takeaway: Prior vs. Likelihood Dominance

With **large** $n$, the likelihood dominates:

$$E(p \mid x) = \frac{\alpha + x}{\alpha + \beta + n} \;\xrightarrow{n \to \infty}\; \frac{x}{n} = \hat{p}_{\text{MLE}}$$

. . .

With **small** $n$, the prior matters:

- Acts as a regulariser — shrinks the estimate toward prior beliefs
- Equivalent to $\alpha + \beta$ additional pseudo-observations before collecting data

. . .

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
This is why **prior sensitivity analysis** is standard practice: vary the prior, check how much the posterior changes. Robust results = the data are doing the work.
:::

---

## Common Conjugate Families

| Likelihood | Parameter | Conjugate Prior | Posterior | Posterior Mean |
|------------|----------|-----------------|-----------|----------------|
| Binomial$(n, p)$ | $p$ | Beta$(\alpha, \beta)$ | Beta$(\alpha + x,\; \beta + n - x)$ | $\frac{\alpha + x}{\alpha + \beta + n}$ |
| Poisson$(\lambda)$ | $\lambda$ | Gamma$(\alpha, \beta)$ | Gamma$(\alpha + \sum x_i,\; \beta + n)$ | $\frac{\alpha + \sum x_i}{\beta + n}$ |
| Normal$(\mu, \sigma^2)$, $\sigma^2$ known | $\mu$ | Normal$(\mu_0, \tau_0^2)$ | Normal$(\mu_n, \tau_n^2)$ | Weighted avg of $\mu_0$ and $\bar{x}$ |
| Normal$(\mu, \sigma^2)$, $\mu$ known | $\sigma^2$ | Inv-Gamma$(\alpha, \beta)$ | Inv-Gamma$\!\left(\alpha + \tfrac{n}{2},\; \beta + \tfrac{1}{2}\sum(x_i-\mu)^2\right)$ | $\frac{\beta + \tfrac{1}{2}\sum(x_i-\mu)^2}{\alpha + \tfrac{n}{2} - 1}$ |
| Exponential$(\lambda)$ | $\lambda$ | Gamma$(\alpha, \beta)$ | Gamma$(\alpha + n,\; \beta + \sum x_i)$ | $\frac{\alpha + n}{\beta + \sum x_i}$ |
| Multinomial$(n, \mathbf{p})$ | $\mathbf{p}$ | Dirichlet$(\boldsymbol{\alpha})$ | Dirichlet$(\boldsymbol{\alpha} + \mathbf{x})$ | $\frac{\alpha_k + x_k}{\sum_j \alpha_j + n}$ |
| Neg. Binomial$(r, p)$ | $p$ | Beta$(\alpha, \beta)$ | Beta$(\alpha + nr,\; \beta + \sum x_i)$ | $\frac{\alpha + nr}{\alpha + \beta + nr + \sum x_i}$ |

---

## Limits of Conjugate Priors

Conjugate families are analytically convenient but **not always appropriate**:

- The prior family is chosen for mathematical convenience, not necessarily scientific reasons
- Many likelihoods (logistic regression, multilevel models) have **no conjugate prior**
- Real prior knowledge may not fit any standard parametric form

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
When conjugacy fails, we need to evaluate $p(x)$ numerically (**usually MCMC or LaPlace approximation**).
:::


# Summary {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## What We Covered

::: nonincremental
1. **Bayes' theorem** — derived from the definition of conditional probability; not a new axiom
2. **Diagnostic test** — how the prior (prevalence) determines the posterior; base-rate neglect
3. **From events to parameters** — $p(\theta \mid x) \propto p(x \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)$; the normalising constant problem
4. **Maximum likelihood** — full analytic derivation, Fisher information, R implementation with `optim()`
5. **MLE vs. Bayesian** — asymptotic caveats, the likelihood principle, credible vs. confidence intervals
6. **Posterior summaries** — mean, median, mode and their loss functions; credible intervals
7. **Conjugate priors** — Beta-Binomial in detail; update rules; prior dominance; conjugate families table
:::

. . .

**Next lecture:** When conjugacy fails → Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)

---

## References

::: nonincremental
- Held, L. & Sabanés Bové, D. (2014). *Applied Statistical Inference: Likelihood and Bayes*. Springer.
- Gimenez, O. (2025). *Bayesian statistics with R*. [oliviergimenez.github.io/bayesian-stats-with-R](https://oliviergimenez.github.io/bayesian-stats-with-R/)
:::
